cards.text: std::unique_ptr is defined in the {{c1::<memory>}} header.
To create a unique_ptr, use: {{c1::std::unique_ptr<Type> ptr = std::make_unique<Type>(args);}}

cards.front: What ownership model does std::unique_ptr implement?
cards.back: Exclusive ownership. Only one std::unique_ptr can own a resource at any time. The resource is automatically deleted when the unique_ptr goes out of scope.

cards.question: What's the preferred way to create a unique_ptr in C++14 and later?
cards.answer: Option B with std::make_unique is preferred.
cards.explanation: std::make_unique is safer (exception-safe), more concise, and avoids explicit new. It was introduced in C++14.

cards.text: std::unique_ptr cannot be {{c1::copied}} (copy constructor/assignment are deleted), but it can be {{c2::moved}} using std::move.

cards.question: How do you transfer ownership of a unique_ptr?
cards.code:
auto ptr1 = std::make_unique<int>(42);
auto ptr2 = /* what goes here? */;
cards.explanation: After this, ptr1 is nullptr and ptr2 owns the resource. std::move is required to transfer ownership.

Use std::unique_ptr when:
  • You need exclusive ownership of dynamically allocated memory
  • You want automatic cleanup
  • You're replacing raw owning pointers
  • Returning dynamically allocated objects from functions
Default choice for smart pointers.
examples: // widget automatically deleted when out of scope

cards.text: Access the object through unique_ptr using {{c1::*ptr}} (dereference) or {{c2::ptr->member}} (arrow operator), just like raw pointers.

cards.front: When should you use the get() method of std::unique_ptr?
cards.back: Use get() when you need to pass the raw pointer to a function that takes a raw pointer parameter but doesn't take ownership. Never delete the pointer returned by get().

cards.question: How do you create a unique_ptr for an array?
cards.explanation: Use Type[] syntax for arrays. Access with arr[i]. However, prefer std::vector or std::array over unique_ptr<T[]> in most cases.

cards.text: Call {{c1::ptr.reset()}} to delete the managed object and set the unique_ptr to {{c2::nullptr}}. You can also call {{c3::ptr.reset(new_ptr)}} to replace the managed object.

cards.concept: Advantages of std::unique_ptr over raw pointers
cards.explanation:
std::unique_ptr advantages:
  • Automatic memory management (RAII)
  • Exception-safe
  • No manual delete needed
  • Clear ownership semantics
  • No overhead in release builds
  • Move-only type prevents accidental copies
